coredata.dc:title: Structural characterization of carbon quantum dots derived from tea residue and their photocatalytic application in CQDs-modified Al2(SO4)3 nanoparticles for sustainable pesticide degradation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Rahmah, Andi Sitti
Heryanto, Heryanto
Rinovian, Asnan
Yudasari, Nurfina
Tahir, Dahlang
coredata.dc:description: Effect of Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) on the photocatalyst performance of Al2(SO4)3/CQDs composites. CQDs were synthesized from tea residue using a hydrothermal method, which has been studied. The investigation included a comprehensive nanostructure analysis through X-ray Diffraction (XRD), Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM), Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), UV–Vis Spectroscopy, and Photoluminescence (PL) Analysis. TEM revealed the formation of coral-shaped clusters in the Al2(SO4)3/CQDs composite, with nanoparticle sizes ranging from 2 to 10 nm. Notably, the Al2(SO4)3/CQDs II variant, comprising 95 % Al2(SO4)3 and 5 % CQDs, demonstrated the highest photocatalytic performance. This composite effectively degraded fipronil pesticide, achieving an impressive 84 % degradation within 60 min. This performance can be attributed to several factors, including the narrowed d-spacing, the work effectiveness of hydroxyl radical active species, the lowest bandgap, and the profound impact on C–C bonds within Al2(SO4)3/CQDs II. The findings suggest that the Al2(SO4)3/CQDs composite holds great promise as an advanced material for photocatalytic pesticide degradation, thereby contributing to the broader goal of ensuring sustainable access to clean water resources. This study provides valuable insights into the design and development of advanced photocatalytic materials, with the potential to significantly impact the field of water purification and environmental sustainability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalyst
Carbon quantum dots
Aluminum sulfate
Pesticide

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) and Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the process of synthesizing CQDs from tea residue (b) Results of irradiating CQDs with UV light in this study (c) Fluorescent luminescence mechanism of CQDs with different color depends on the bandgap and degrees of surface oxidation from Ref. [22] for comparison. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration of the synthesis process of Al2(SO4)3/CQDs in this study.
(a) SEM image of pure CQDs (b) diameter size distribution histogram data of pure CQDs (c) SEM image for Al₂(SO₄)₃/CQDs composite.
TEM micrographs of CQDs with magnifications (a) 10.000X (b) 20.000X (c) 30.000X (d) particle size distribution histogram data.
XRD spectra (a) full spectra for all samples (b) enlarger spectra for angle diffraction spectra at 18 to 22.o
FTIR spectra (a) comparison of spectra for different compositions (b) different arrangements for accessible identified peak and comparison for all composites.
(a) UV–Vis spectra of pesticide for all samples (b) Photocatalytic degradation (e) Langmuir-Hinshelwood models (d) Photoluminescence analysis.
(a) Change in pesticide concentration (b) percentage degradation from free radical trapping experiments.
Schematic illustration of the degradation process of pesticide using Al2(SO4)3/CQDs composites.
Composition of each sample in the study.
Crystallite size and dislocation density analysis from the XRD spectra based on their peaks of the diffraction spectra (2 Theta), miller index (hkl).
Functional groups exist in Al2(SO4)3/CQDs composite.
Adsorption and photocatalysis coefficient from the Langmuir-Hinshelwood model.

Comparison of photocatalyst performance between composites containing Al2(SO4)3 and those containing CQDs, including pollutant types, methods, and materials used during their preparation from various references for comparison in this study.

head.ce:title: Structural characterization of carbon quantum dots derived from tea residue and their photocatalytic application in CQDs-modified Al2(SO4)3 nanoparticles for sustainable pesticide degradation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Hasanuddin University, Makassar, 90245, Indonesia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Hasanuddin University, Makassar 90245, Indonesia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Mining Technology, National Research and Innovation Agency, Lampung, 35361, Indonesia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Center for Mining Technology, National Research and Innovation Agency, Lampung 35361, Indonesia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Photonics, National Research and Innovation Agency, South Tangerang, 15314, Indonesia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Center for Photonics, National Research and Innovation Agency, South Tangerang 15314, Indonesia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Effect of Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) on the photocatalyst performance of Al2(SO4)3/CQDs composites. CQDs were synthesized from tea residue using a hydrothermal method, which has been studied. The investigation included a comprehensive nanostructure analysis through X-ray Diffraction (XRD), Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM), Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), UV–Vis Spectroscopy, and Photoluminescence (PL) Analysis. TEM revealed the formation of coral-shaped clusters in the Al2(SO4)3/CQDs composite, with nanoparticle sizes ranging from 2 to 10 nm. Notably, the Al2(SO4)3/CQDs II variant, comprising 95 % Al2(SO4)3 and 5 % CQDs, demonstrated the highest photocatalytic performance. This composite effectively degraded fipronil pesticide, achieving an impressive 84 % degradation within 60 min. This performance can be attributed to several factors, including the narrowed d-spacing, the work effectiveness of hydroxyl radical active species, the lowest bandgap, and the profound impact on C–C bonds within Al2(SO4)3/CQDs II. The findings suggest that the Al2(SO4)3/CQDs composite holds great promise as an advanced material for photocatalytic pesticide degradation, thereby contributing to the broader goal of ensuring sustainable access to clean water resources. This study provides valuable insights into the design and development of advanced photocatalytic materials, with the potential to significantly impact the field of water purification and environmental sustainability.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Environmental pollution, especially water pollution, is a critical problem in the world as it relates to the quality of life of humans and their environment [1]. Urban water pollution is exacerbated by the expansion of social structures and the wide scope of industrial production. Uncontrolled discharge of industrial effluents has led to water pollution in many countries, as carcinogenic components are found in dyes from garment and food factories. This poses potential health risks to livestock, plants, and humans. Water pollution must be handled carefully because of its impact on the environment, and in the long run, it also affects the bodies of living things. Based on reference [2,3] reported that about four billion people worldwide do not have access to clean water, resulting in millions of deaths each year. This situation is attributed to water pollutants such as metals and pesticides. The use of pesticides, which play an essential role in agricultural food production, continues to increase yearly [4,5]. Pesticides are ranked the second largest contributor to wastewater pollution, posing a high risk to humans and animals [5].

Researchers worldwide are actively seeking solutions by introducing photocatalyst materials that can be easily applied in society by naturally harnessing sunlight's power. Photocatalyst technology is widely recognized as promising for mitigating such problems. As an environmentally friendly, cost-effective, and efficient technology, photocatalysts can convert solar power into chemical energy while degrading pollutants under ambient operating temperature and pressure conditions [6].

Material that has been reported for photocatalyst applications is Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) [7]. CQDs have been extensively studied as potential materials for photocatalyst applications. Among the non-metallic elements, carbon is considered the most suitable choice due to its abundance and presence as a major component in the molecular structure of living organisms. It has a ligand at the surface state and is rich in amino, hydroxyl, carboxyl, and other organic functional groups. CQDs have outstanding properties such as very small size (less than 10 nm) [8–10], excellent water solubility, low toxicity, cost-effectiveness, and stable fluorescence performance. Therefore, it can be used in many fields, such as biological imaging [9–11], organic analysis, photocatalysts, and clinical medicine [12–14]. The most important is the ability to exhibit visible fluorescence under ultraviolet light irradiation [8 15].

Aluminum sulfate Al2(SO4)3 was used in this study as a substrate because it shows fast precipitation properties. It is also abundant in nature and has a low cost compared to commonly used photocatalyst materials such as ZnO and TiO2 [13]. Another advantage is the durability and stability of AL2(SO4)3, making it able to maintain the catalytic process, and this material can also be used as a metal ion binder to reduce the electron-hole recombination process in the sample [14]. Even though a few studies still report using Al2(SO4)3 as a catalyst material doped by CQDs to improve photocatalytic efﬁciency. Here, we introduce CQDs from tea residue using a hydrothermal method because it has several advantages, including low cost, ease of obtaining, and reduced tea dreg waste [15,16]. Several studies have also proved CQDs from tea dregs to have high capacity and good cycle stability [17,18]. Mixing these two materials can certainly increase the effectiveness and efficiency of the photocatalysis process. CQDs can modify the AL surface to enhance light absorption and better electron transfer[19–21]. Furthermore, to determine the characteristics of this material, a comprehensive nanostructural analysis was carried out using various techniques, including X-ray Diffraction, Scanning Electron Microscopy, Transmission Electron Microscopy, Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy, and UV–Vis Spectroscopy. Our primary aim is to investigate the impact of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) on the performance of the composite Al2(SO4)3/CQDs photocatalyst. This study will identify and highlight the specific composition (Al2(SO4)3/CQDs) that exhibits the highest photocatalytic performance in degrading fipronil pesticides, also explore the factors contributing to the superior photocatalytic performance, mainly focusing on the impact on d-spacing and C–C bonds within Al2(SO4)3/CQDs. Thus, this study proposes Al2(SO4)3/CQDs composite as a potential advanced material for photocatalytic degradation of pesticides to support sustainable clean water resources and environmental protection.

The CQDs were synthesized from tea residue. aluminium sulfate (Al2(SO4)3) (Merck, 98.2 %), aquabidest, HCL (Merck), Fipronil Pesticide (Sigma-Aldrich, ≥94.0 %), Isopropanol (IPA) (PureChem, 99.9 %), Ethylene Diamine Tetraacetic Acid (EDTA) (Loba Chemie, 99.4 %), p-Benzoquinone (BQ) (Chemsavers, 99 %).

Tea residue obtained from household use is used as raw material for CQD. This tea residue contains many organic compounds from tea leaves that have the potential and advantages previously described to be used as CQDs. The tea residue was dried, pulverized with a mortar, and filtered with a 200-mesh. 5 g of tea residue was dissolved into 30 ml of distilled water using a magnetic stirrer at 200 rpm for 30 min, and HCl was added until it reached pH 5. After that, it sonicated for 20 min. The resulting solution was transferred to a Teflon lined-steel autoclave and heated in a furnace at 165 °C for 12 h. Afterward, it was filtered with 125 mm diameter filter paper. The resulting solution was then centrifuged at 1000 rpm for 100 min. The CQDs solution was then irradiated with an ultraviolet (UV) laser to determine whether CQDs were successfully synthesized from tea residue. CQDs were declared successful if they fluoresced when irradiated with UV light. Then, the fluorescent CQDs are ready for the next stage; the results and fluorescent illustrations of CQDs during irradiation by UV light can be seen in Fig. 1 (a and b). Fig. 1 (c) shows the relation between the bandgap and the luminescence color of CQDs with different degrees of surface oxidation from Ref. [22] for comparison in this study.

CQDs and Al2(SO4)3 with certain percentages, as shown in Table 1 , were dissolved into distilled water with a constant volume of 5 ml using a magnetic stirrer at 100 rpm for 1 h. The resulting solution was then put into a furnace to be heated at 39 °C for 4 h. After that, it was dried in the sun at room temperature for 48 h. It is pulverized with a mortar and filtered with 100 mesh when it hardens. The scheme of the Al2(SO4)3/CQDs preparation procedure can be seen in Fig. 2 .

The hydrolysis reactions and its elucidation of aluminium sulfate (alum) are as follows.
1.Basic Aluminum sulfate hydrolysis: Hydrolysis occurs when alum (Al2(SO4)3) is introduced to water. The aluminium ions from alum (Al3+) react with water molecules, leading to the formation of aluminium hydroxide (Al(OH)3) and sulfuric acid (H2SO4) by chemical reaction process as follows:
(1) Al2(SO4)3 + 6H2O → 2Al(OH)3 + 3H2SO4

In this reaction, each aluminium ion (Al3+) bonds with three hydroxide ions (OH−) to create one molecule of aluminium hydroxide, a solid precipitate separating from the solution. Simultaneously, sulfuric acid is produced as a byproduct.
2.
Neutralization of aluminium hydroxide: Aluminum hydroxide (Al(OH)3), once formed, can engage in another reaction if excess hydroxide ions are present in the solution. This process leads to the formation of soluble complex ions called hexa-hydroxoaluminate ions ([Al(OH6]3-) by the reaction as follows:
(2) Al(OH)3 + 3OH− → [Al(OH)6]3-

In this reaction, aluminium hydroxide ions (Al(OH)3) interact with hydroxide ions (OH−) to produce hexa-hydroxide-aluminate ions ([Al(OH)6]3-), which remain dissolved in the solution. These complex ions are stable and prevent the reformation of aluminium hydroxide.

The elucidation of these reactions showcases the transformation of aluminium sulfate into aluminium hydroxide, a solid precipitate, followed by the interaction of aluminium hydroxide with excess hydroxide ions, forming soluble complex ions. These reactions are integral to water purification and wastewater treatment, where the flocculation and removal of impurities rely on the formation and properties of aluminium hydroxide.

The samples were characterised by X-ray Diffraction (XRD Shimadzu 7000) in the 2θ range from 15° to 50° to determine the crystal structure of a sample. Fourier Transforms Infra-Red (FTIR) Shimadzu Corp type with measurement samples in the 500-4000 cm−1 wave number range to determine the functional groups. Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) to analyze the microstructure of the sample. The surface morphology and distribution of CQDs were investigated by Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM). The absorbance of pollutants neutralized by the composites was observed by UV–visible spectroscopy with a wavelength range of 180–700 nm. Photoluminescence analysis was performed using a PerkinElmer Lambda S55 spectrofluorometer at a wavelength of 325 nm.

A photocatalyst test was conducted using a degradation pesticide (Fipronil (C12H4Cl2F6N4OS)) as a pollutant, which is a widely used pesticide in Indonesia. About 3 g of Al2(SO4)3/CQDs composite material was dissolved into 100 ml of pesticide solution (pesticide concentration was 30 %) at room temperature and then irradiated with a halogen lamp (300 W, OSRAM 645, Germany) 30 cm away from the pesticide solution. Free radical trapping experiments were also carried out by adding IPA, EDTA, and BQ liquids to each container. The conditions applied were adjusted to the experimental conditions for pesticide degradation. The test was conducted from 0 to 60 min, where the characterization was performed every 10 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) and Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis
ce:section.ce:para:
Figs. 3 and 4
display the surface morphology of CQDs synthesized by hydrothermal method at 165 °C for 12 h. Fig. 3 (a) was taken using SEM to view the surface morphology of the pure CQDs. The image shows a homogeneous surface morphology with refined grains. Fig. 3 (b) shows the size distribution of CQDs granules with an average diameter of 0.015 μm. Fig. 3 (c) shows the SEM image of the composite sample, which shows the CQDs particles distributed on the Al surface. The CQDs will modify the surface to conduct the photocatalyst process effectively and efficiently. Fig. 4 (a, b, c) shows the TEM image of the CQDs with different magnifications, and Fig. 4 (d) shows their size distribution. It can be seen from the image that the products are homogeneously dispersed nanoparticles. At a low magnification of 500 nm (Fig. 4 (b)), coral-shaped particles with an average diameter of 10 nm are visible, and at a high magnification of 50 nm, spherical particles with an average diameter of 3.3 nm are visible (Fig. 4 (d)). This follows the ideal size of CQDs, 2–10 nm. Carbonization is related to hydrothermal temperature: with increasing temperature, higher carbonization occurs. High carbonization compacts the carbon particles, resulting in small-size CQDs [23]. In the case of semiconductor nanoparticles, a reduction in particle size will increase the band gap [24].

The particle size of CQDs is certainly very influential on their performance in degrading pesticides, where the small particle size will increase the ability of CQDs in the degradation process. Because its small size allows CQDs to have a highly effective surface area, the more significant the interaction with pesticide molecules, the greater the adsorption and degradation [25,26]. The absorption of the light spectrum, especially visible light, will also be wider due to this small size [27]. Thus, increasing electron transfer, which will impact the formation of reactive particles to break down pesticide molecules, will also increase [28]. Thus, its addition to Al2(SO4)3 will increase the efficiency and effectiveness of pesticide degradation.

XRD determines the identification of the crystal structure phase. The XRD patterns of Al2(SO4)3 and Al2(SO4)3/CQDs with varying compositions are shown in Fig. 5 (a) . The XRD results show that the five samples match Al2SO4(OH)4ꞏ5H2O XRD pattern. Reorientation or splitting of crystals may occur, which may lead to different peak intensities at the same position obtained for the samples [29]. Diffraction peaks were obtained at 2θ = 19.52°, 20.4°, 22.3°, 24.2°, 26.3, 29.4°, 33.8° and 35.7° with indices (101), (111), (2
1 ‾
1), (1
0 ‾
2), (1
4 ‾
1), (141), (311), (061) and (110) respectively based on JCPDS file No. 00-020-0060. No characteristic peaks for CQDs are observed in the Al2(SO4)3/CQDs pattern because the CQDs are evenly distributed, and their diffraction intensity is relatively lower than that of Al2(SO4)3. The existence of CQDs in the sample needs to be further verified by FTIR.

Fig. 5 (b) shows a peak shift due to the addition of CQDs in the composite, which shows structural changes. Peak with crystal orientation (101) shifts to a lower angle due to more CQDs concentration, and at crystal orientation (111) shifts to a larger angle due to less CQDs concentration. The reduced d spacing in Al2(SO4)3/CQDs II indicates the distance between the constituent atoms is reduced, consequently increasing the stabilization of bonds, which leads to improved photocatalyst performance [30]. In addition, shift and widening peaks are related to crystallite size, which is related to increasing surface area, increasing adsorption capability, and directly impacting the improved oxidation process of the target molecule [31]. The crystallite size (
D
) of Al2(SO4)3/CQDs was calculated from the Debye-Scherrer equation as follows:
(3)
D =
k λ
β
cos
θ
Where
D
is the crystallite size,
k
is the Scherrer constant (0.9), λ is the wavelength for X-ray radiation (λ = 0.154 nm), β is the Full Width at Half Maximum (FWHM), and
θ
is the X-ray diffraction angle. The average crystallite size and dislocation density can be seen in Table 2 . It can be seen that increasing the concentration of CQDs from 2 % to 15 % reduces the crystal size. The smallest average crystallite size obtained was 9 nm. In addition, the average crystallite size of the CQDs doped Al2(SO4)3 composite is smaller than the pure Al2(SO4)3, indicating that CQDs doping significantly affects crystallization in the photocatalyst [32]. Crystallite size depends on various factors such as intensity, peak broadening, sharpness, dislocation density, and strain. Irregularities or cracks in the crystal structure cause dislocation density. An increase in dislocation density increases strain, reducing the crystallite size. The following equation calculates dislocation density [33]:
(4)
δ =
1
D 2

Fig. 6 shows the FTIR spectra of the composite in this study for the wavenumber range 500–4000 cm−1 to identify the functional groups in the composite. Based on the FTIR spectra, the functional groups identified can be seen in Table 3 . The FTIR spectra of each composite in this study are shown in Fig. 6(a and b) for full and identical spectra with different arrangements for easy comparison. The preparation of the FTIR spectrum in Fig. 6 aims to show the effect of adding CQDs in Al2(SO4)3. The more significant the peak detected, the stronger the functional group detected at that wave number. The spectrum at wavenumber 931 cm−1 is attributed to Al–O–Al, and at 2962 cm−1, the C–H stretching of tert-butyl occurs. The CC bond was confirmed at wavenumber 1647 cm−1, showing an increase following the addition of CQDs and the C–H bond at wavenumber 707 cm−1. The adsorption peak at wavenumber 601 cm−1 is attributed to Al–OH vibration. At wavenumber 1101 cm−1, the C–C bond is confirmed to increase sharply with the addition of CQDs in the Al2(SO4)3/CQDs composite. C–C bonds in adsorbent molecules can become reaction targets in photocatalysts and increase the reactivity of pollutants to photocatalysts.

When adsorbent molecules have C–C bonds that are easily activated, degradation reactions tend to be faster and more efficient [34,35]. The data shown in Fig. 6 shows that the Al2(SO4)3/CQDs II sample has the largest peak of C–C bonds, indicating that the sample has good potential in degrading pollutants. The peak at wavenumber 3421 cm−1 is attributed to O–H stretching. Its intensity gets more robust with the addition of CQDs, indicating the presence of more hydroxyl groups on the surface of CQDs doped Al2(SO4)3 photocatalyst. The hydroxyl groups play an essential role in the photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants [32]. These results confirmed that CQDs successfully bonded with Al2(SO4)3 in the Al2(SO4)3/CQDs composite.

The photocatalytic activity of each composite by degradation of pesticide solution under 300 W halogen lamp and the corresponding results are given in Fig. 7 (a) . The degradation of pesticides was monitored by measuring the solution's absorbance. Almost all the composites have good photocatalytic properties, which can degrade the pesticide by up to 83 % in just 60 min, as seen in Fig. 7 (b). A higher and faster degradation percentage was achieved by the Al2(SO4)3/CQDs II samples with (Al2(SO4)3:CQDs ratio of 19:1, which degraded the pesticide up to 84 % within 60 min, indicating CQDs affect the photocatalytic performance of the composite.

The Langmuir-Hinshelwood kinetic model was used to know and describe the photocatalytic activity of the samples. Fig. 7 (c) shows the graph of the ratio of the reciprocal of rate (1/r) to pollutant concentration (1/C) and its linear line to validate this kinetic model. Where the Langmuir-Hinshelwood kinetic equation is as follows [44]:
(5a)
r o
= −
d c
d t
=
k r
K
C
e q
1 + K
C
e q
r o
is the initial rate of reaction (mg/L.min),
k r
is the rate constant for photocatalysis (mg/L.min),
K
is the rate constant for adsorption (L/mg),
C
e q
is the concentration of bulk solution (mg/L) at equilibrium adsorption condition,
c
is the concentration of the bulk solution at time
t
. While the liner equation used is as follows [45]:
(6)
1
r o
=
1
k r
+
1
k r
K
C
e q

In photocatalytic reactions, the
K
value can be obtained from calculations on the slope value of the linear line. The value of air
K r
is obtained from calculations on the same line intercept value [46], and the data of each sample are shown in Table 4 . The data show that the CQDs II sample has the largest adsorption constant value (0.115 L/mg), which supports the previous result that this sample has the highest degradation performance.

Furthermore, to study the splitting activity and lifetime of electron-hole pairs generated by photogeneration, photoluminescence (PL) analysis was used, and the results are shown in Fig. 7 (d). The maximum emission of all composite samples is seen at 420 nm, while for pure Al2(SO4)3 at 480 nm. All composite samples also have a lower PL intensity than pure Al2(SO4)3, indicating that adding CQDs to the samples is beneficial in enhancing the formation of electron-hole pairs and increasing the release of reactions in the valence and conduction bands in generating radical ions [47]. These PL results also correspond to the results of the sample degradation performance, where the CQDs II sample has the lowest PL intensity value even though the amount of CQDs addition is more than the CQDs III and IV samples. Based on these results, it is found that excessive addition of CQDs can accelerate electron-hole recombination, which will reduce the photocatalytic activity in degrading pollutants. Therefore, adding the right amount of CQDs to Al2(SO4)3 is necessary to increase the formation and reduce the recombination of electron-hole pairs.

Al2(SO4)3/CQDs composite enhances photogenerated electron-hole pairs so that the transfer of the electron-hole pair mechanism becomes a more effective mean, which improves the photocatalytic performance [48]. The rapid separation of electron-hole pairs avoids the initial recombination of the photocatalysts. This indicates that CQDs play an essential role in the transport of the electron-hole pair excitation properties of the materials. Al2(SO4)3/CQDs IV showed slow degradation, possibly due to radicals failing to spit out the chemical bonds resulting from recombining the chemical bonds of pollutants that have been broken down previously [5].

In photocatalysis, there are 3 active species responsible for the breakdown of pollutant molecules, including holes (h+), hydroxyl radicals (•OH), and superoxide radicals (•O2 −). Free radical trapping experiments were conducted to determine the charge transfer mechanism and which active species are most influential during degradation [49]. This experiment used IPA, EDTA, and BQ as scavengers of •OH, h+, and •O2 − traps, respectively. The sample with the highest degradation value is CQDs II. Fig. 8 shows the results of adding the capture solution that will reduce the percentage of degradation, which is 23.13 %, 67.52 %, and 75.94 % for IPA, EDTA, and BQ, respectively. So, it can be obtained that hydroxyl radicals (•OH) play a major role in the degradation of pesticide molecules using photocatalysts from Al2(SO4)3/CQDs composites judging from the significant decrease in the degradation percentage when adding IPA.
For comparison, photocatalyst performance from various materials and synthesis methods can be seen in Table 5 .

Adding CQDs to the Al2(SO4)3 matrix increases UV–vis absorption at all wavelengths, thereby increasing the availability of visible light, stimulating the formation of more charge carriers, and enhancing photocatalytic activity [64]. Fig. 9 shows the energy gap (
E g
) of Al2(SO4)3/CQDs was calculated by the Kubelka-Munk method from the quantitative analysis of UV–Vis spectra by using relations:
(5b)
(
α h v
)
2
= A
(
h v − E g
)
where
α
is the light absorption index,
h
is Planck's constant,
v
is the frequency,
E g
is the band gap energy and
A
is the absorbance. The band gaps for Al2(SO4)3/CQDs I, II, III, and IV are 3.35 eV, 2.21 eV, 3.31 eV, and 3.32 eV, respectively. It can be seen that sample Al2(SO4)3/CQDs II has an anomaly due to a drastic decrease in its energy bandgap. This supports the results of the previous characterization, where sample Al2(SO4)3/CQDs II is a prominent sample. Reduction in d spacing from the XRD results and large functional groups from the FTIR results. This supports why the degradation obtained is the highest using sample Al2(SO4)3/CQDs II. The increased light-responsive region for the lowest band gap energy of the Al2(SO4)3/CQDs photocatalyst will help absorb photons and generate more electron holes. As a trap of electrons, the CQDs reduce the recombination of electrons and holes, which can be attributed to increasing the photocatalytic activity.

The possible mechanism of photocatalytic pesticide degradation by Al2(SO4)3/CQDs can be seen by schematic illustration in Fig. 10 . The photon of sunlight having energy comes to composite and transfer their energy to electrons (e–) in the valence band (VB) of Al2(SO4)3, and then e− will have power for excitation to the conduction band (CB), leaving a hole (h+) in VB, the e− is readily oxidised by dissolved oxygen in water to form •O2 −. In addition, holes can oxidise H2O molecules adsorbed on the surface of Al2(SO4)3 to produce •OH− [4].

The addition of CQDs can effectively enhance the absorption of visible light and facilitate charge transfer. As a result, many e− and h+ are generated, and the CQDs trap electrons to avoid recombination with the holes, consequently increasing radical production and photocatalyst performance [26]. Therefore, this mechanism will increase radical ions h+, •O2 − and •OH, which react with pesticide molecules and produce non-toxic CO2 and H2O. The complete degradation mechanism of pesticides by chemical reaction is as follows:
(6) Al2(SO4)3/CQDs + hv → Al2(SO4)3/CQDs [h+ (VB) + e− (CB)]
(7) Al2(SO4)3/CQDs [h+ (VB) + e− (CB)] → Al2(SO4)3/CQDs (h+ (VB)) + Al2(SO4)3/CQDs (e− (CB))
(8) Al2(SO4)3/CQDs (e−) + O2 → O2 −
(9) Al2(SO4)3/CQDs (h+) + H2O → •OH + H+
(10) •OH + pesticide → harmless products + CO2 + H2O

This study investigated the effect of CQDs on the photocatalytic activity of Al2(SO4)3/CQDs composite. CQDs particles were synthesized using the hydrothermal method. According to the XRD data, the addition of CQDs influences the crystallite size, which gets smaller with the addition of CQDs, and there is a narrowed d spacing in Al2(SO4)3/CQDs II indicates crystallite size and the distance between the constituent atoms is reduced to increase the photocatalytic process. Based on the FTIR analysis of all samples, Al2(SO4)3/CQDs II has the most profound C–C bond, which in pollutant molecules can be the target of reaction in photocatalyst and could increase the reactivity of pollutants enhance the efficiency of photocatalyst performance. As a result of the incorporation of CQDs nanoparticles, the band gap of Al2(SO4)3 is reduced from 6.04 eV to 3.35 eV and 2.21 eV in Al2(SO4)3/CQDs I and Al2(SO4)3/CQDs II, respectively. The narrowed band gap leads to less energy for electrons to enter the conduction band. For those reasons, Al2(SO4)3/CQDs II is the best sample with the highest pesticide degradation with 84 % in 60 min because it yields narrowed d-spacing, lowest transmittance for C–C bond, and narrowed band gap. The results obtained have also been validated by PL analysis, where an increase in charge separation can occur by adding the right amount of CQDs. Furthermore, free radical trapping experiments showed that hydroxyl radical (•OH) is the most active sepsis involved in the degradation of pesticide molecules. The decrease in photocatalytic performance on Al2(SO4)3/CQDs III and Al2(SO4)3/CQDs IV are due to the high recombination process between electron and hole in CQDs and Al2(SO4)3.

From this study, the Al2(SO4)3/CQDs composite is an advanced material that is very promising in the photocatalytic application of pesticide degradation in water. This will contribute to wider utilization, namely maintaining the quality of clean water available. Further research is needed to develop composite material with CQDs as an advanced photocatalyst material to find the most effective composite material to degrade various samples. Ultimately, this research will significantly impact the development of materials for water purification and environmental sustainability.

Andi Sitti Rahmah: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Conceptualization. Heryanto Heryanto: Writing – original draft, Conceptualization. Asnan Rinovian: Methodology, Conceptualization. Nurfina Yudasari: Methodology, Conceptualization. Dahlang Tahir: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization.
Data available on request for reasonable reason.
Not Applicable.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Thanks to the Materials and Energy Lab, Physics Department, and Hasanuddin University members for valuable discussion.
